B.mismatch: Libraries expect tabular records and need a thin adapter from query-cue objects.
B.name: Structured fuzzy entity resolution

C.mismatch: No first-class action-layer abstention label; unresolved must be wrapped around zero/low-score outputs.
C.name: Canonical gazetteer matching

D.mismatch: Context-generated blocking is not novel by itself and must be compared to ordinary blocking baselines.
D.name: Candidate blocking

E.mismatch: Native interfaces are tracking-centric, so structured symbolic observations require a representation adapter.
E.name: Streaming observation-to-entity association

F.mismatch: Not a canonical tabular gazetteer matcher; best fit is sequential sensor-style association.
F.name: Multi-hypothesis identity tracking

G.mismatch: Whole architectures are much larger than the current seam and do not natively satisfy typed external authority contracts.
G.name: Cue-based episodic retrieval

H.mismatch: Patterns exist, but not as a drop-in Python component for mixed ER/tracking pipelines.
H.name: Context-biased declarative retrieval

I.mismatch: No single audited library owns both symbolic event provenance and downstream action authority.
I.name: Temporal belief projection

J.mismatch: Authority boundary across heterogeneous native systems is integration-specific, but not enough to justify a new matcher.
J.name: Uncertainty-preserving commit authority

K.mismatch: Full provisional-identity lifecycle crosses ER, tracking, and memory governance and is outside current benchmark scope.
K.name: Open-world new-entity lifecycle

L.mismatch: Audited libraries expose native matchers or trackers, but none provides a shared external context/routing/budget interface with authority-preserving fallback across heterogeneous mechanisms.
L.name: Cross-mechanism routing and budget allocation

overall_interpretation: Only the cross-mechanism controller seam survives; native matchers and trackers should be adopted or wrapped.
